content: With respect to deadlocks in an operating system, which of the following statements is/are FALSE?

options.content: Banker's algorithm is used to prevent deadlocks
Deadlock formation can be prevented by ensuring that the hold and wait condition is not allowed
An assignment edge in a resource allocation graph is marked from a process to a resource
A safe state guarantees that all processes can finish without formation of a deadlock

explanation:
(a) Banker's algorithm ensures that the system never enters an unsafe state by carefully allocating resources. This technique is classified under deadlock avoidance, not prevention. So, option (a) is false.

(b) Deadlock requires four necessary conditions: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. If the hold and wait condition is eliminated, one of the necessary conditions is removed. Hence, deadlock cannot occur. So, option (b) is true.

(c) In a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG):

  • A request edge goes from process → resource.

  • An assignment edge goes from resource → process. Thus, the given statement reverses the direction. So, option (c) is false.

(d) By definition, in a safe state, there exists a safe sequence in which all processes can complete execution. Therefore, deadlock will not occur. So, option (d) is true.

content: Consider a system consisting of $k$ instances of a resource $R$, being shared by 5 processes. Assume that each process requires a maximum of two instances of resource $R$ and a process can request or release only one instance at a time. Further, a process can request the second instance of the resource only after acquiring the first instance. The minimum value of K for the system to be deadlock-free is $\_\_\_\_$ . (answer in integer)
explanation:
For deadlock to happen, each of the $5$ processes must hold one instance of resource $R$ and wait for one more instance.

Since each process needs at most $2$ instances:

  • A process can first take $1$ instance.

  • Then it may request the second instance.

  • Deadlock occurs if all processes hold $1$ instance each and no free instance is left.

So, if there are $5$ instances total:

  • All $5$ processes can each hold $1$ instance.

  • No instance remains free.

  • Each process waits for one more instance.

  • Hence, deadlock is possible.

To avoid deadlock, after giving one instance to each of the $5$ processes, at least $1$ extra instance must still be available so that one process can complete and release resources.

Thus,

$$ k \geq 5 + 1 = 6 $$

Therefore, the minimum value of $k$ is

$$ \boxed{6} $$

content:
Consider the following threads, T1, T2 and T3 executing on a single processor, synchronized using three binary semaphore variables, S1, S2 and S3, operated upon using standard wait( ) and signal( ). The threads can be context switched in any order and at any time.

$${T_1}$$ $${T_2}$$ $${T_3}$$
while (true) {
wait ($${S_3}$$);
print ("C");
signal ($${S_2}$$); }		 while (true) {
wait ($${S_1}$$);
print ("B");
signal ($${S_3}$$); }		 while (true) {
wait ($${S_2}$$);
print ("A")
signal ($${S_1}$$); }

Which initialization of the semaphores would print the sequence BCABCABCA....... ?

options.content: S<sub>1</sub> = 1; S<sub>2</sub> = 1; S<sub>3</sub> = 1
S<sub>1</sub> = 1; S<sub>2</sub> = 1; S<sub>3</sub> = 0
S<sub>1</sub> = 1; S<sub>2</sub> = 0; S<sub>3</sub> = 0
S<sub>0</sub> = 1; S<sub>2</sub> = 1; S<sub>3</sub> = 1

explanation:
In order to get the required output only semaphore S1 should be initialized to 1, other semaphore should be initialized to 0.

So, S1 = 1; S2 = 0; S3 = 0

Answer is option (c).

content: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to deadlocks?

options.content: Circular wait is a necessary condition for the formation of deadlock.
In a system where each resource has more than one instance, a cycle in its wait for graph indicates the presence of a deadlock.
If the current allocation of resources to processes leads the system to unsafe state, then deadlock will necessarily occur.
In the resource-allocation graph of a system, if every edge is an assignment edge, then the system is not in deadlock state.

explanation:
(a) Circular wait is one of the necessary condition for the formation of deadlock hence it is true.

(b) If the resource has more than one instance then cycle is just a necessary condition but not the sufficient condition, hence it is false.

(c) Unsafe state will not always leads to deadlock, because it depends on behaviour of the processes, hence it is false.

(d) If every edge is only allocation, it means there is no requirement of the resources, so deadlock not possible, hence it is true.

content: Consider a system with $$3$$ processes that share $$4$$ instances of the same resource type. Each process can request a maximum of $$K$$ instances. Resource instances can be requested and released only one at a time. The largest value of $$K$$ that will always avoid deadlock is ____.
A system has 6 identical resources and N processes competing for them. Each process can request atmost 2 resources. Which one of the following values of N could lead to a deadlock?
A computer has six tape drives, with n processes competing for them. Each process may need two drives. What is the maximum value of n for the system to be deadlock free?

$P=\left\{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\right\}$ consists of all active processes in an operating system.

$R=\left\{R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4\right\}$ consists of single instances of distinct types of resources in the system.

The resource allocation graph has the following assignment and claim edges.

Assignment edges: $R_1 \rightarrow P_1, R_2 \rightarrow P_2, R_3 \rightarrow P_3, R_4 \rightarrow P_4$ (the assignment edge $R_1 \rightarrow P_1$ means resource $R_1$ is assigned to process $P_1$, and so on for others) Claim edges: $P_1 \rightarrow R_2, P_2 \rightarrow R_3, P_3 \rightarrow R_1, P_2 \rightarrow R_4, P_4 \rightarrow R_2$ (the claim edge $P_1 \rightarrow R_2$ means process $P_1$ is waiting for resource $R_2$, and so on for others) Which of the following statement(s) is/are CORRECT?

options.content: Aborting $P_1$ makes the system deadlock free.
Aborting $P_3$ makes the system deadlock free.
Aborting $P_2$ makes the system deadlock free.
Aborting $P_1$ and $P_4$ makes the system deadlock free.

explanation:
Let's analyze the situation step by step.

We have:

• Processes: $$P=\{P_1,P_2,P_3,P_4\}$$

• Resources: $$R=\{R_1,R_2,R_3,R_4\}$$

The current assignment edges (resource is assigned to process) are:

$$R_1 \rightarrow P_1,\quad R_2 \rightarrow P_2,\quad R_3 \rightarrow P_3,\quad R_4 \rightarrow P_4$$

And the claim (or request) edges are:

$$P_1 \rightarrow R_2,\quad P_2 \rightarrow R_3,\quad P_3 \rightarrow R_1,\quad P_2 \rightarrow R_4,\quad P_4 \rightarrow R_2$$

A useful way to see potential deadlocks is to build a wait‐for graph (WFG). In the WFG, a directed edge exists from process $$P_i$$ to process $$P_j$$ if $$P_i$$ is waiting for some resource that is currently held by $$P_j$$. Let’s list these edges:

From $$P_1 \rightarrow R_2$$ and since $$R_2$$ is held by $$P_2$$, we have:

  $$P_1 \rightarrow P_2.$$

From $$P_2 \rightarrow R_3$$ and $$R_3$$ is with $$P_3$$:

  $$P_2 \rightarrow P_3.$$

From $$P_3 \rightarrow R_1$$ and $$R_1$$ is with $$P_1$$:

  $$P_3 \rightarrow P_1.$$

This creates a cycle:

  $$P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_1.$$

Also, from $$P_2 \rightarrow R_4$$ (since $$R_4$$ is with $$P_4$$) we get:

  $$P_2 \rightarrow P_4.$$

And from $$P_4 \rightarrow R_2$$ (with $$R_2$$ held by $$P_2$$):

  $$P_4 \rightarrow P_2.$$

This gives a cycle between $$P_2$$ and $$P_4$$.

Thus, there are two deadlock cycles:

One cycle involving $$P_1, P_2, P_3$$.

Another cycle between $$P_2$$ and $$P_4$$.

Now, let’s examine the options one by one.

──────────────────────────────

Option A: Aborting $$P_1$$

──────────────────────────────

• Aborting $$P_1$$ means that $$P_1$$ is removed and the resource $$R_1$$ (which $$P_1$$ held) becomes free.

• Effects on the WFG:

  - The edge $$P_3 \rightarrow R_1$$ (which depended on $$R_1$$ being held by $$P_1$$) is eliminated.

  - However, the cycle between $$P_2$$ and $$P_4$$ still remains because:

    $$P_2 \rightarrow R_4 \text{ (held by }P_4\text{)}$$ and

    $$P_4 \rightarrow R_2 \text{ (held by }P_2\text{)}.$$

Thus, aborting $$P_1$$ alone does not break the deadlock.

──────────────────────────────

Option B: Aborting $$P_3$$

──────────────────────────────

• Aborting $$P_3$$ frees up $$R_3$$.

• In the WFG:

  - The edge $$P_2 \rightarrow R_3$$ is removed (since $$R_3$$ is free).

  - However, the cycle between $$P_2$$ and $$P_4$$ remains, from:

    $$P_2 \rightarrow R_4 \text{ (held by }P_4\text{)}$$ and

    $$P_4 \rightarrow R_2 \text{ (held by }P_2\text{)}.$$

So aborting $$P_3$$ does not remove all deadlocks.

──────────────────────────────

Option C: Aborting $$P_2$$

──────────────────────────────

• Aborting $$P_2$$ frees resource $$R_2$$, and removes all edges involving $$P_2$$.

• The remaining allocation is:

  - $$P_1$$ holds $$R_1$$.

  - $$P_3$$ holds $$R_3$$.

  - $$P_4$$ holds $$R_4$$, and the freed $$R_2$$ is available.

• The remaining claim edges:

  - $$P_1 \rightarrow R_2$$: Now $$R_2$$ is free, so it does not induce a wait-for edge.

  - $$P_3 \rightarrow R_1$$ persists, which gives:

    $$P_3 \rightarrow P_1.$$

  - $$P_4 \rightarrow R_2$$ is also not an edge since $$R_2$$ is free.

The only remaining waiting relation is $$P_3 \rightarrow P_1$$, which by itself does not form a cycle.

Thus, aborting $$P_2$$ makes the system deadlock free.

──────────────────────────────

Option D: Aborting $$P_1$$ and $$P_4$$

──────────────────────────────

• Aborting $$P_1$$ and $$P_4$$ frees up resources $$R_1$$ and $$R_4$$.

• The remaining processes:

  - $$P_2$$ holds $$R_2$$.

  - $$P_3$$ holds $$R_3$$.

• The remaining claim edges:

  - $$P_2 \rightarrow R_3$$ gives an edge from $$P_2$$ to $$P_3$$ (since $$R_3$$ is with $$P_3$$).

  - $$P_3 \rightarrow R_1$$ is dropped because $$R_1$$ is now free.

There is now just a single edge from $$P_2$$ to $$P_3$$, and no cycles exist in the wait‐for graph.

Thus, aborting $$P_1$$ and $$P_4$$ also makes the system deadlock free.

──────────────────────────────

Conclusion

──────────────────────────────

The analysis shows that:

• Option C (aborting $$P_2$$) and

• Option D (aborting both $$P_1$$ and $$P_4$$)

are correct ways to break all cycles in the resource allocation graph and make the system deadlock free.

The final answer is: Option C and Option D.

content:
Consider a multi-threaded program with two threads T1 and T2. The threads share two semaphores: s1 (initialized to 1) and s2 (initialized to 0). The threads also share a global variable x (initialized to 0). The threads execute the code shown below.

// code of T1

wait(s1);

x = x + 1;

print(x);

wait(s2);

signal(s1);

// code of T2

wait(s1);

x = x + 1;

print(x);

signal(s2);

signal(s1);

Which of the following outcomes is/are possible when threads T1 and T2 execute concurrently?

options.content: T1 runs first and prints 1, T2 runs next and prints 2
T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 runs next and prints 2
T1 runs first and prints 1, T2 does not print anything (deadlock)
T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 does not print anything (deadlock)

explanation:
Let’s consider potential outcomes when T1 and T2 execute concurrently.

Analysis:

Initially, s1 = 1 and both threads perform a wait operation on s1. This allows only one thread to proceed at any given time.

If T1 runs first, it will:

Execute wait(s1) and proceed since s1 is initially 1 (this decrements s1 to 0).

Increment x from 0 to 1 and print 1.

Attempt to execute wait(s2). However, since s2 is 0, it gets blocked.

At this point, T1 is blocked at wait(s2). Meanwhile, T2 cannot run because it will get blocked on wait(s1), as s1 is 0. This scenario leads to a deadlock.

Conversely, if T2 runs first, it will:

Execute wait(s1) and proceed, decrementing s1 to 0.

Increment x from 0 to 1 and print 1.

Execute signal(s2), incrementing s2 to 1.

Execute signal(s1), incrementing s1 to 1.

Now, T1 can proceed:

Execute wait(s1) and proceed, decrementing s1 to 0.

Increment x from 1 to 2 and print 2.

Attempt to execute wait(s2) and succeed since s2 is 1, then decrement s2 to 0.

Execute signal(s1), incrementing s1 to 1.

This scenario does not lead to a deadlock, and both threads print their results sequentially.

Conclusion:

The possible outcomes are:

T2 runs first, prints 1, and then T1 runs, printing 2.

T1 runs first, prints 1, leading to a deadlock, preventing T2 from printing anything.

Hence, the correct outcomes are:

T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 runs next and prints 2.

T1 runs first and prints 1, T2 does not print anything (deadlock).

content:
Consider the following pseudocode, where S is a semaphore intialized to 5 in line#2 an counter is a shared variable intialized to 0 in line#1. Assume that the increment operation in line#7 is not atomic.

1. int counter = 0;

2. Semaphore S = init(5);

3. void parop(void)

4. {

5. wait (S);

6. wait (S);

7. counter++;

8. signal (S);

9. signal (S);

10. }

If five threads execute the function parop concurrently, which of the following program behavior (s) is/are possible?

options.content: There is a deadlock involving all the threads.
The value of counter is 5 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.
The value of counter is 1 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.
The value of counter is 0 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.

explanation:
To determine which program behaviors are possible when five threads execute the function parop concurrently, we need to analyze the code and consider how the threads interact, especially with respect to the semaphore S and the shared variable counter.

Given Code:

1. int counter = 0;

2. Semaphore S = init(5);

3. void parop(void)

4. {

5.     wait(S);

6.     wait(S);

7.     counter++;

8.     signal(S);

9.     signal(S);

10. }

Initial Values:

counter is initialized to 0.

Semaphore S is initialized to 5.

Note: The increment operation counter++ at line 7 is not atomic.

Analysis of Each Option:

Option A: There is a deadlock involving all the threads.

Possibility of Deadlock:

Understanding Semaphores:

A semaphore S is a counter that controls access to shared resources.

The wait(S) operation decrements the semaphore. If S is zero, the process blocks until S becomes positive.

The signal(S) operation increments the semaphore and wakes up waiting processes if any.

Execution Flow:

Each thread performs two wait(S) operations and two signal(S) operations.

Total wait(S) operations: $ 5 \text{ threads} \times 2 = 10 $

Total signal(S) operations: $ 5 \text{ threads} \times 2 = 10 $

Semaphore S starts at 5.

Scenario Leading to Deadlock:

All threads execute their first wait(S); successfully, decrementing S from 5 to 0.

When threads attempt the second wait(S);, S is 0, so they all block.

No thread can proceed to signal(S); because they're blocked at the second wait(S);.

Result: A deadlock involving all threads occurs.

Conclusion: Option A is possible.

Option B: The value of counter is 5 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.

Possibility of counter Being 5:

Avoiding Deadlock:

With proper scheduling, threads can avoid deadlock.

For example, one thread completes both wait(S); operations, increments counter, and performs both signal(S); operations before the next thread starts.

Execution Flow Without Deadlock:

Thread T1 acquires semaphore twice (S goes from 5 to 3), increments counter, and releases semaphore twice (S back to 5).

This process repeats for each thread.

Counter Increment:

Each thread increments counter once.

If counter++ operations are executed without interference, counter will be incremented five times.

Non-Atomic counter++:

Since counter++ is not atomic, race conditions can occur.

However, if threads execute counter++ sequentially (due to semaphore protection), counter can correctly become 5.

Conclusion: Option B is possible.

Option C: The value of counter is 1 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.

Possibility of counter Being 1:

Race Conditions Due to Non-Atomic Increment:

Even with semaphore protection, if multiple threads reach counter++ simultaneously (possible due to incorrect semaphore usage or context switches), race conditions can cause lost increments.

Scenario Leading to counter Being 1:

All threads reach counter++ at the same time.

Due to non-atomicity, all threads read counter as 0, increment it to 1, and write back 1.

Result: Despite five increments, counter ends up being 1.

Conclusion: Option C is possible.

Option D: The value of counter is 0 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.

Possibility of counter Being 0:

Analyzing the Increments:

For counter to remain 0, all increments would need to be completely lost.

This would require that none of the counter++ operations have any effect.

Is This Possible?

While race conditions can cause some increments to be lost, it's unlikely (practically impossible) for all increments to be lost completely.

At least one increment operation would succeed, increasing counter by at least 1.

Conclusion: **Option D is *not* possible**.

Final Answer:

Possible Behaviors:

Option A: Deadlock involving all threads.

Option B: counter equals 5 after successful execution.

Option C: counter equals 1 after successful execution.

Impossible Behavior:

Option D: counter remains 0 after successful execution.

Therefore, the possible program behaviors are Options A, B, and C.

Answer: Options A, B, and C are possible.

content: Consider the following snapshot of a system running $n$ concurrent processes. Process $i$ is holding $X_i$ instances of a resource $\mathrm{R}, 1 \leq i \leq n$. Assume that all instances of R are currently in use. Further, for all $i$, process $i$ can place a request for at most $Y_i$ additional instances of R while holding the $X_i$ instances it already has. Of the $n$ processes, there are exactly two processes $p$ and $q$ such that $Y_p=Y_q=0$. Which one of the following conditions guarantees that no other process apart from $p$ and $q$ can complete execution?

options.content: $X_p + X_q < \min \{Y_k \mid 1 \leq k \leq n, k \neq p, k \neq q\}$
$X_p + X_q < \max \{Y_k \mid 1 \leq k \leq n, k \neq p, k \neq q\}$
$\min (X_p, X_q) \geq \min \{Y_k \mid 1 \leq k \leq n, k \neq p, k \neq q\}$
$\min (X_p, X_q) = \max \{Y_k \mid 1 \leq k \leq n, k \neq p, k \neq q\}$

explanation:
We have $ n $ concurrent processes and a single type of resource $ R $. Let's denote :

$ X_i $: The number of instances of resource $ R $ currently held by process $ i $.

$ Y_i $: The maximum additional number of instances of $ R $ that process $ i $ may still request before it can complete.

All instances of $ R $ are currently allocated (i.e., there are no free instances available). Among these $ n $ processes, exactly two processes, $ p $ and $ q $, have $ Y_p = Y_q = 0 $. This means processes $ p $ and $ q $ need no further resources to finish their execution.

What happens when $ p $ and $ q $ complete?

Since $ p $ and $ q $ need no additional resources, they can eventually complete and release their currently held resources. Once both $ p $ and $ q $ have completed, they will release a total of $ X_p + X_q $ instances of $ R $.

After $ p $ and $ q $ finish, the system will have $ X_p + X_q $ free instances of $ R $ available for other processes.

Condition for other processes to complete :

For any other process $ k $ (where $ k \neq p, q $) to complete, it must be able to acquire enough resources to meet its maximum additional request $ Y_k $. If it cannot acquire at least $ Y_k $ more resources, it will remain blocked and cannot complete.

Therefore, if after $ p $ and $ q $ release their resources, no single process $ k $ can obtain its required $ Y_k $ resources, then no other processes can complete.

Identifying the critical condition :

The worst-case scenario for allowing at least one more process (other than $ p $ and $ q $) to complete is that we need :

$ X_p + X_q \geq \min \{ Y_k \mid k \neq p, q \} $

so that at least one other process $ k $ can get the resources it needs and eventually finish.

If this inequality does NOT hold — that is :

$ X_p + X_q < \min \{ Y_k \mid k \neq p, q \}, $

then even after $ p $ and $ q $ finish and release all their resources, the available resources $ X_p + X_q $ are still insufficient to fulfill the smallest additional request $ Y_k $ of any other process $ k $. Hence, no other process can proceed to completion.

Matching with the given options :

Among the provided options, the one that directly states this condition is :

Option A : $ X_p + X_q < \min \{ Y_k \mid 1 \leq k \leq n, k \neq p, k \neq q \} $.

This ensures that no other process can ever get enough resources to continue, thus guaranteeing that no other process apart from $ p $ and $ q $ can complete execution.

Correct Answer : Option A

content:
In a system, there are three types of resources: $$E, F$$ and $$G.$$ Four processes $${P_0},$$ $${P_1},$$ $${P_2}$$ and $${P_3}$$ execute concurrently. At the outset, the processes have declared their maximum resource requirements using a matrix named Max as given below. For example, Max$$\left[ {{P_{2,}}F} \right]$$ is the maximum number of instances of $$F$$ that $${{P_{2,}}}$$ would require. The number of instances of the resources allocated to the various processes at any given state is given by a matrix named Allocation.

Consider a state of the system with the Allocation matrix as shown below, and in which $$3$$ instances of $$E$$ and $$3$$ instances of $$F$$ are the only resources available.

Allocation
E F G
P0 1 0 1
P1 1 1 2
P2 1 0 3
P3 2 0 0

Max
E F G
P0 4 3 1
P1 2 1 4
P2 1 3 3
P3 5 4 1

From the perspective of deadlock avoidance, which one of the following is true?

options.content: The system is in $$safe$$ state.
The system is not in $$safe$$ state, but would be $$safe$$ if one more instance of $$E$$ were available
The system is not in $$safe$$ state, but would be $$safe$$ if one more instance of $$F$$ were available
The system is not in $$safe$$ state, but would be $$safe$$ if one more instance of $$G$$ were available

explanation:
To determine if the system is in a safe state, we use the Banker's Algorithm.

First, we need to calculate the Need matrix, which represents the remaining resources each process could request.

Need[i, j] = Max[i, j] - Allocation[i, j]

Given:

Allocation Matrix:

    E  F  G

P0  1  0  1

P1  1  1  2

P2  1  0  3

P3  2  0  0

Max Matrix:

    E  F  G

P0  4  3  1

P1  2  1  4

P2  1  3  3

P3  5  4  1

Calculate Need Matrix:

  • P0_Need: [4-1, 3-0, 1-1] = [3, 3, 0]

  • P1_Need: [2-1, 1-1, 4-2] = [1, 0, 2]

  • P2_Need: [1-1, 3-0, 3-3] = [0, 3, 0]

  • P3_Need: [5-2, 4-0, 1-0] = [3, 4, 1]

So, the Need matrix is:

    E  F  G

P0  3  3  0

P1  1  0  2

P2  0  3  0

P3  3  4  1

Next, we are given the Available resources:

Available = [3 (for E), 3 (for F), 0 (for G)]

(The problem states "3 instances of E and 3 instances of F are the only resources available," implying G has 0 available instances).

Now we apply the safety algorithm:

  1. Initialize Work = Available = [3, 3, 0]

  2. Initialize Finish = [false, false, false, false] for P0, P1, P2, P3.

We look for a process Pi such that Finish[i] is false AND Need[i] <= Work. If found, we execute Pi, update Work, and set Finish[i] = true. We repeat until no such process can be found, or all processes are finished.

Iteration 1:

  • Current Work = [3, 3, 0]

  • Check P0: Need[P0] = [3, 3, 0]. Is [3, 3, 0] <= [3, 3, 0]? Yes.

    • Execute P0.

    • Work = Work + Allocation[P0] = [3, 3, 0] + [1, 0, 1] = [4, 3, 1]

    • Finish[P0] = true

    • Safe sequence so far: <P0>

Iteration 2:

  • Current Work = [4, 3, 1]

  • Check P1: Need[P1] = [1, 0, 2]. Is [1, 0, 2] <= [4, 3, 1]? No, because Need[P1, G] = 2 is greater than Work[G] = 1. P1 cannot execute yet.

  • Check P2: Need[P2] = [0, 3, 0]. Is [0, 3, 0] <= [4, 3, 1]? Yes.

    • Execute P2.

    • Work = Work + Allocation[P2] = [4, 3, 1] + [1, 0, 3] = [5, 3, 4]

    • Finish[P2] = true

    • Safe sequence so far: <P0, P2>

Iteration 3:

  • Current Work = [5, 3, 4]

  • Check P1: Need[P1] = [1, 0, 2]. Is [1, 0, 2] <= [5, 3, 4]? Yes.

    • Execute P1.

    • Work = Work + Allocation[P1] = [5, 3, 4] + [1, 1, 2] = [6, 4, 6]

    • Finish[P1] = true

    • Safe sequence so far: <P0, P2, P1>

  • Check P3: Need[P3] = [3, 4, 1]. Is [3, 4, 1] <= [5, 3, 4]? No, because Need[P3, F] = 4 is greater than Work[F] = 3. P3 cannot execute yet.

Iteration 4:

  • Current Work = [6, 4, 6]

  • Check P3: Need[P3] = [3, 4, 1]. Is [3, 4, 1] <= [6, 4, 6]? Yes.

    • Execute P3.

    • Work = Work + Allocation[P3] = [6, 4, 6] + [2, 0, 0] = [8, 4, 6]

    • Finish[P3] = true

    • Safe sequence so far: <P0, P2, P1, P3>

All processes (P0, P1, P2, P3) have been marked true in Finish. Therefore, a safe sequence exists (<P0, P2, P1, P3>), and the system is in a safe state.

The final answer is $\boxed{A}$

content: Consider a non-negative counting semaphore $$S.$$ The operation $$P(S)$$ decrements $$S,$$ and $$V(S)$$ increments $$S.$$ During an execution, $$20$$ $$P(S)$$ operations and $$12$$ $$V(S)$$ operations are issued in some order. The largest initial value of $$S$$ for which at least one $$P(S)$$ operation will remain blocked is _____________ .

Consider the following policies for preventing deadlock in a system with mutually exclusive resources.

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm I}.$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ Processes should acquire all their resources at the beginning of execution. If
$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ any resource is not available, all resources acquired so far are released
$$\,\,\,\,\,{\rm II}.$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ The resources are numbered uniquely, and processes are allowed to request
$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ for resources only in increasing resource numbers
$$\,\,\,{\rm III}.$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ The resources are numbered uniquely, and processes are allowed to request
$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ for resources only in decreasing resource numbers
$$\,\,\,{\rm IV}.$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ The resources are numbered uniquely. A process is allowed to request only
$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ for a resource with resource number larger than its currently held resources

Which of the above policies can be used for preventing deadlock?

options.content: Any one of $${\rm I}$$ and $${\rm III}$$ but not $${\rm II}$$ or $${\rm IV}$$
Any one of $${\rm I},$$ $${\rm III},$$ and $${\rm IV}$$ but not $${\rm II}$$
Any one of $${\rm II}$$ and $${\rm III}$$ but not $${\rm I}$$ or $${\rm IV}$$
Any one of $${\rm I},$$ $${\rm II},$$ $${\rm III},$$ and $${\rm IV}$$

The producer will be able to add an item to the buffer, but the consumer can never
consume it.
The consumer will remove no more than one item from the buffer.

Deadlock occurs if the consumer succeeds in acquiring semaphore s when the buffer is
empty.
The starting value for the semaphore n must be 1 and not 0 for deadlock-free operation.

content:
An operating system uses the Banker’s algorithm for deadlock avoidance when managing the allocation of three resource types X, Y, and Z to three processes P0, P1, and P2. The table given below presents the current system state. Here, the Allocation matrix shows the current number of resources of each type allocated to each process and the Max matrix shows the maximum number of resources of each type required by each process during its execution. There are 3 units of type X, 2 units of type Y and 2 units of type Z still available. The system is currently in a safe state. Consider the following independent requests for additional resources in the current state:

REQ1: P0 requests 0 units of X, 0 units of Y and 2 units of Z
REQ2: P1 requests 2 units of X, 0 units of Y and 0 units of Z

Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.content: Only REQ1 can be permitted.
Only REQ2 can be permitted.
Both REQ1 and REQ2 can be permitted.
Neither REQ1 nor REQ2 can be permitted

content: Consider a system with 4 types of resources R1 (3 units), R2 (2 units), R3 (3 units), R4 (2 units). A non-preemptive resource allocation policy is used. At any given instance, a request is not entertained if it cannot be completely satisfied. Three processes P1, P2, P3 request the sources as follows if executed independently. Which one of the following statements is TRUE if all three processes run concurrently starting at time t = 0?

options.content: All processes will finish without any deadlock
Only P1 and P2 will be in deadlock
Only P1 and P3 will be in deadlock
All three processes will be in deadlock

content: Which of the following is NOT true of deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance schemes?

options.content: In deadlock prevention, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe.
In deadlock avoidance, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe.
Deadlock avoidance is less restrictive than deadlock prevention.
Deadlock avoidance requires knowledge of resource requirements a priori.

content: A single processor system has three resource types X, Y and Z, which are shared by three processes. There are 5 units of each resource type. Consider the following scenario, where the column alloc denotes the number of units of each resource type allocated to each process, and the column request denotes the number of units of each resource type requested by a process in order to complete execution. Which of these processes will finish LAST?

options.content: P0
P1
P2
None of the above, since the system is in a deadlock.

content:
Consider the following snapshot of a system running n processes. Process i is
holding x<sub>i</sub> instances of a resource R, for $$1 \le i \le n$$. Currently, all instances of R are
occupied. Further, for all i, process i has placed a request for an additional
y<sub>i</sub> instances while holding the x<sub>i</sub> instances it already has. There are exactly two
processes p and q such that y<sub>p</sub> = y<sub>q</sub> = 0. Which one of the following can serve as
a necessary condition to guarantee that the system is not approaching a deadlock?

options.content: $$\min ({x_p},{x_q}) < {\max _{k \ne p,q}}{y_k}$$
$${x_p} + {x_q} \ge {\min _{k \ne p,q}}{y_k}$$

content: Suppose n processes, P<sub>1</sub>,......., P<sub>n</sub> share m identical resource units, which can be reserved and released one at a time. The maximum resource requirement of process P<sub>i</sub> is S<sub>i</sub>, where S<sub>i</sub> > 0. Which one of the following is a sufficient condition for ensuring that deadlock does not occur?

options.content: $$\forall i,{s_i} < m$$
$$\forall i,{s_i} < n$$
$$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^n {{s_i}}  < \left( {m + n} \right)$$
$$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^n {{s_i}}  < \left( {m \times n} \right)$$

content: Which of the following is NOT a valid deadlock prevention scheme?

options.content: Release all resources before requesting a new resource
Number the resources uniquely and never request a lower numbered resource than the last one requested.
Never request a resource after releasing any resource
Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.

content: An operating system contains 3 user processes each requiring 2 units of resource R.The minimum number of units of R such that no deadlocks will ever arise is

Consider a system having m resources of the same type. These resources are
shared by 3 processes A, B and C, which have peak demands of 3, 4 and 6
respectively. For what value of m deadlock will not occur?

A computer system has 6 tape drives, with n process completing for them.
Each process may need 3 tape drives. The maximum value of n for which the
system is guaranteed to be deadlock free is:

A computer system uses the Banker’s Algorithm to deal with deadlocks. Its current state is shown in the tables below, where P0, P1, P2 are processes and R0, R1, R2 are resource types.
(a) Show that the system can be in this state.
(b) What will the system do on a request by process P0 for one unit of resource type R1?
answer:
(a) System is in the safe state.The safe state is "P1,P2,P0"
(b) By granting the request of P0, the system goes into unsafe state so system would delay the request and wait till resources are available.

content:
Consider the resource allocation graph given in the figure.
(a) Find if the system is in a deadlock state.
(b) Otherwise, find a safe sequence.
answer:
(a) No, The system is not in the deadlock state.
(b) Safe sequence -> P2 - P0 - P1 - P3
